CVR Cleaning Quality Analysis

Jack Mulqueeney

2026-07-08

1 Introduction

In this note, I summarise the CVR number cleaning process for the KFST and OpenTender datasets. I also describe the name-matching process and provide some examples of how names are cleaned and matched. I also provide summary tables of the cleaning and matching results for both datasets.

2 Process overview

Both datasets follow the same main process. OpenTender needs one extra check for winner names that may contain several firms.

1. Clean source CVRs
→
2. Expand multiple winners
→
3. Find candidates with a name but no CVR
→
4. Match names to the CVR register
→
5. Keep the match or send it to review

OpenTender only Before fuzzy matching, identify rows that might contain multiple firm names and split them if necessary.

3 Matching process

The process follows the approach in Bisnode matching documentation_V7. It starts with strict exact matches. It then makes the names more general. Rows that match are removed before the next step. The same cleaning is applied to the winner names and to the registered main names and binames in the CVR keys.

This section illustrates how the matching names are created in the winner data.

3.1 Step 1: winner_name_basic

The first matching name is called winner_name_basic. To get this name, I

  1. Convert to lowercase
  2. Remove spaces at the start and end of the string
  3. Replace Danish and other accented letters with a common spelling
  4. Standardise legal forms, after detecting them as a standalone term (i.e. not as part of another word)

The keys for step (3) and (4) are below.

Letter standardisation
Original Replacement
ø oe
æ ae
å aa
ö oe
ä ae
á or à a
é or è e
Legal-form standardisation
Terms.recognised Stored.firm.type
A/S, AS, A/, aktieselskab, aktieselskabet, GmbH a/s
ApS, anpartsselskab, anpartsselskabet, AB aps
I/S i/s
K/S, KS k/s
IVS ivs
P/S p/s
AMBA, A.M.B.A., A M B A amba
SMBA, S.M.B.A. smba
FMBA, F.M.B.A. fmba

If no legal form is found, the firm type is stored as Undetermined.

3.2 Steps 2, 3, and 5: winner_name_match and winner_name_no_spaces

The next stage follows step 2 of the original documentation. It makes the following character changes:

  • oe becomes o, and aa becomes a.
  • & becomes og.
  • v/ becomes a space, while other slashes are removed.
  • Hyphens and parentheses become spaces.
  • Commas, periods, colons, and quotation marks are removed.
  • Repeated spaces are collapsed.

The script also standardises selected whole words. It only replaces complete words. It does not replace text found inside a longer word.

Whole-word standardisation
Original.words Replacement
international int
and og
av, of af
limited ltd
denmark, danmark dk
holdings holding
sweden, sverige se
corporation corp
company, comp co
copenhagen, kobenhavn, cph, kopenhamn kbh
i, ii, iii 1, 2, 3

This set of cleaning produces:

  1. winner_name_match, which keeps spaces and is used in fuzzy step 5.
  2. winner_name_no_spaces, which is winner_name_match after removing spaces. This match variable is used for exact matching in steps 2 and 3.

3.3 Steps 4 and 6: winner_name_broad

Exact step 4 and fuzzy step 6 use winner_name_broad. This variable is created from winner_name_match, but in addition:

  1. Removes common words with little information about firm identity.
  2. Sorts remaining words alphabetically.
  3. Joins words without spaces.

Common words removed: af, arhus, asset, assets, broderna, brdr, brodrerne, co, dansk, data, development, dk, ejendomsselskabet, finans, forsikring, group, holding, hotels, hotel, int, invest, kbh, komplementaerssaelskabet, livforsikringsselskab, management, media, nordic, nordisk, og, scandinavia, scandinavian, se, service, services, skandia, software, system, systems, ltd, filial, and for.

The four prepared name fields therefore have distinct uses:

Prepared name fields used in matching
Prepared.field Cleaning.level Used.in
winner_name_basic Lowercase; accented letters standardised; legal form extracted; extra spaces removed Exact step 1
winner_name_no_spaces Built from winner_name_match; all spaces removed Exact steps 2 and 3
winner_name_match Built from winner_name_basic; punctuation and selected words standardised; spaces retained Fuzzy step 5
winner_name_broad Built from winner_name_match; common words removed; remaining words sorted and joined Exact step 4 and fuzzy step 6

3.4 Check against the original documentation

The current R code follows the documented core process. It applies the same letter changes, legal-form extraction, character changes, whole-word replacements, space removal, common-word removal, and word sorting.

The current implementation adds a few conservative safeguards:

  • It recognises FMBA as an additional legal form.
  • A fuzzy match is not accepted when several CVRs share the highest score.
  • Repeated appearances of the same CVR are collapsed during candidate ranking. If the same prepared name points to different CVRs, those CVRs are retained and the match is marked as ambiguous instead of silently dropping one.
Name-matching stages
Stage Prepared.name Comparison Purpose
Exact 1 winner_name_basic Basic name and firm type Find names that already agree.
Exact 2 winner_name_no_spaces Prepared name without spaces and firm type Allow punctuation and spacing differences.
Exact 3 winner_name_no_spaces Prepared name without spaces Allow the firm type to differ.
Exact 4 winner_name_broad Broad name Remove common words and ignore word order.
Fuzzy 5 winner_name_match Prepared main names and binames Allow small spelling differences. The score must be above 85.
Fuzzy 6 winner_name_broad Broad main names and binames Use the broad name. Main names must score above 86. Binames must score above 89.
Review — Remaining or uncertain rows Check fuzzy, ambiguous, and unmatched rows by hand.

3.5 Real examples of name cleaning

The table below uses two winner names from the KFST matching candidates. OpenTender uses the same four name fields before its extra multiple-firm checks.

Exact steps 2 and 3 use the same cleaned name. Step 2 also requires the firm type to match. Step 3 ignores the firm type.

Real examples of names used in the four exact-match steps
Original winner name Step Name used for matching Firm type rule
XL-Byg Vorup Tømmerhandel & Byggecenter A/S Exact 1 xl-byg vorup toemmerhandel & byggecenter Must match a/s
XL-Byg Vorup Tømmerhandel & Byggecenter A/S Exact 2 xlbygvoruptommerhandelogbyggecenter Must match a/s
XL-Byg Vorup Tømmerhandel & Byggecenter A/S Exact 3 xlbygvoruptommerhandelogbyggecenter Ignored
XL-Byg Vorup Tømmerhandel & Byggecenter A/S Exact 4 bygbyggecentertommerhandelvorupxl Ignored
Struensee & Co. Management Consulting P/S Exact 1 struensee & co. management consulting Must match p/s
Struensee & Co. Management Consulting P/S Exact 2 struenseeogcomanagementconsulting Must match p/s
Struensee & Co. Management Consulting P/S Exact 3 struenseeogcomanagementconsulting Ignored
Struensee & Co. Management Consulting P/S Exact 4 consultingstruensee Ignored

Potential matches must also fit the tender date. The registered name can start or end up to two years before or after the tender date. When several matches remain, the script prefers:

  1. A main registered name over a biname.
  2. A name active on the tender date.
  3. The oldest registered name.

3.6 Simple examples

These examples come from the original matching documentation.

Illustrative matching examples
Winner.name Registered.name Result
Electro Oil Int Electro-Oil International Both become ‘electrooilint’. They match at Exact 2.
NGI Holdig NGI Holding The fuzzy score is 95. This is above the step 5 threshold.

4 KFST

4.1 First CVR cleaning

The KFST source has separate fields for the winner CVR, winner name, and winner country. Some notes on the data:

  • The CVR numbers are generally already pretty clean. While many rows feature punctuation, those punctuation marks (in particular the ‘;’) are used to separate multiple winners. The script first checks whether a row has one or several valid CVRs. It then removes repeated CVRs and creates one row for each remaining CVR. After separation, 97 rows had their CVR number standardised (removing white space, hyphens, alphabetical letters, punctuation). More detail available in the table below.

  • A row is treated as a clear single winner when its CVR is exactly eight digits. Spaces can be removed first. Remaining non-missing rows may contain several winners. For these rows, the script splits the CVR, name, and country fields together. It then creates one row for each winner. This keeps each CVR beside the correct name and country.

CVR number standardisation counts after multi-firm separation
flag_cvr_ws flag_cvr_alphabet flag_cvr_punct flag_cvr_standardised
15 77 22 97
KFST Cleaning
Measure Count Share Share of
Original rows identified as single winners 17,181 82.0% Original winner rows
Original rows treated as multiple winners 3,780 18.0% Original winner rows
Winner rows after multiple-winner expansion 11,132 39.3% Final winner rows
Winner rows with a valid CVR after cleaning 25,420 89.8% Final winner rows
Winner rows with a missing CVR after cleaning 2,604 9.2% Final winner rows

4.2 Candidates for name matching

A matching candidate is a Danish firm with a winner name but no cleaned CVR.

There are 801 KFST matching candidates.

4.3 Matching results

Most matches are exact. Fuzzy matches are less common, and some rows remain unmatched.

KFST matching results
Result Count Share
Exact match 629 78.5%
Fuzzy match 57 7.1%
No automatic match 115 14.4%

The next table separates successful matches by the specific matching stage. name_match_method identifies exact or fuzzy matching. name_match_step_code identifies the specific step.

KFST successful matches by matching step
Method Step Matching stage Count Share of matches
Exact 1 exact matching: basic name and firm type 506 73.8%
Exact 2 exact matching: no spaces and firm type 60 8.7%
Exact 3 exact matching: no spaces 53 7.7%
Exact 4 exact matching: broad name 10 1.5%
Fuzzy 5 fuzzy matching: prepared main name 47 6.9%
Fuzzy 6 fuzzy matching: broad biname 1 0.1%
Fuzzy 6 fuzzy matching: broad main name 9 1.3%
KFST rows that need name-match review
Review.reason Count Share
Fuzzy match 57 7.1%
Ambiguous exact match 14 1.7%
No automatic match 115 14.4%

A fuzzy match is not treated as fully confirmed. Fuzzy matches, ambiguous exact matches, and unmatched rows remain in the manual-review file.

4.4 KFST buyer match quality

KFST buyer data do not contain buyer CVRs in the raw source. The buyer matching workflow therefore tries to recover buyer CVRs from the CVR-name key.

There are 42,564 KFST buyer matching candidates.

Status Count Share

5 OpenTender

5.1 First CVR cleaning

OpenTender has more variation in the winner CVR field. It can contain country prefixes, spaces, repeated CVRs, and longer non-Danish identifiers.

The script first extracts distinct eight-digit CVRs. It only standardises delimiters when a row has more than one distinct valid CVR. It then removes repeated CVRs and creates one row for each remaining CVR.

Before name matching, the script can also fill a missing CVR when the same winner name has exactly one valid CVR elsewhere in OpenTender. The source row is kept for audit.

OpenTender first-pass cleaning
Measure Count Share Share of
Original rows with zero or one valid CVR 161,221 99.9% Original source rows
Original rows with several valid CVRs 120 0.1% Original source rows
Winner rows after multiple-CVR expansion 289 0.2% Final winner rows
Missing CVRs filled from the same winner name 14,592 9.0% Final winner rows
Winner rows with a valid CVR before name matching 75,829 47.0% Final winner rows
Winner rows with a missing CVR before name matching 85,681 53.0% Final winner rows

5.2 Candidates for name matching

The OpenTender definition is the same as the KFST definition. A candidate is a Danish winner with a name but no cleaned CVR.

OpenTender can later split one source row into several firms. Candidate counts therefore use distinct source row_id values.

There are 25,316 OpenTender matching candidates.

5.3 OpenTender multiple-firm checks

OpenTender first uses the same four whole-name exact stages as KFST. It then does two extra checks before fuzzy matching.

5.3.1 Remove consortium language

Some rows contain words such as consortium, joint venture, or JV. The script removes this wording from a temporary copy of the full name. It then repeats the four exact matching stages above on this cleaned name.

5.3.2 Test possible name splits

The OpenTender data are ambiguous as to whether and how multiple firm names are separated. Identifying precisely whether a row contains multiple firm names is challenging, since by visual inspection of the data the separators between firms are inconsistent and aren’t obviously not part of a single firm name. For example, the firm name “Stenner & Sons” could plausibly be one firm “Stenner & Sons” or two firms “Stenner” and “Sons”.

Being too generous with identifying possible multi-firm rows is a major risk, since we could have many false positives in the final analysis dataset. To minimise the risk of false positives, I opted for a conservative flag for potential multiple firm status. A row is eligible for multi-firm separation ONLY when:

  • It contains collaboration language (e.g. consortium, joint venture, and other variants); OR
  • It contains at least two legal forms (e.g. A/S and ApS both present in the row).

After identifying candidate rows, I then create all possible splits of the provided firm name at plausible boundaries. The table below illustrates how this process works for the example “Firm A, Firm B & Firm C”.

Partition Example
partition_id partition_code partition_text segment_number segment_text
1 3 Firm A; Firm B; Firm C 1 Firm A
1 3 Firm A; Firm B; Firm C 2 Firm B
1 3 Firm A; Firm B; Firm C 3 Firm C
2 2 Firm A, Firm B; Firm C 1 Firm A, Firm B
2 2 Firm A, Firm B; Firm C 2 Firm C
3 1 Firm A; Firm B & Firm C 1 Firm A
3 1 Firm A; Firm B & Firm C 2 Firm B & Firm C

The script tests possible splits at plausible boundaries (e.g. ampersand, comma, semi-colon, og, etc). It ONLY accepts a split when:

  1. every segment has exactly ONE exact match
  2. every segment maps to a different CVR
  3. only one partition_id satisfies requirements (1) and (2).

For tractability, I make two assumptions. Firstly, firm names appear sequentially from left to right. This means I don’t test the partition “Firm A & Firm C; Firm B” for the example above. Secondly, only rows with at most 5 identified delimiters are tested. This keeps the number of partitions capped at 31.

Unclear rows stay unchanged and are sent off for review.

OpenTender multiple-firm split results
Measure Count Share Share of
Source rows eligible for the split test 487 1.9% Matching candidates
Source rows with one accepted split 126 25.9% Rows eligible for the split test
Firm rows produced by accepted splits 293 1.1% Winner rows after name matching
Source rows with several possible complete splits 4 0.8% Rows eligible for the split test

The examples below are selected directly from the current accepted results. One example is shown for each available eligibility reason.

Examples of OpenTender winner-name splitting
Why tested Original winner name Accepted split Matched CVRs
collaboration vocabulary Konsortiet Hass/Berg Hass; Berg 35134670; 27562787
collaboration vocabulary and multiple legal forms Konsortiet Grontmij A/S/COWI A/S Grontmij A/S; COWI A/S 48233511; 44623528
multiple legal forms Forstas ApS/Malus ApS Forstas ApS; Malus ApS 29615756; 29615888

The same review rule applies to OpenTender. Fuzzy matches, ambiguous exact matches, and unresolved multiple-firm names still need review.

5.5 OpenTender buyer match quality

OpenTender buyer data often already contain a cleaned buyer CVR. The matching workflow is only used for rows where the buyer CVR is missing but a buyer name is available. The tables below focus on those buyer name-matching candidates.

6 Overall quality review

For a final analysis, I would only fully trust the first three steps of the exact matching procedure. Exact matching recovers many missing CVRs, but the match quality varies substantially by step. Early exact matches that retain firm type look relatively reliable, while later “exact” matches based on broad/generalised names can produce clear false positives, especially for buyers and OpenTender records. Ambiguous and fuzzy matches are correctly flagged for manual review, but they still populate the final CVR columns, so downstream analysis should not use *_cvr_final without filtering or separately handling flag_manual_name_review == TRUE.

7 Other quality analyses

7.1 Potential typoes in CVR names

I’ve noticed that some firm names appear with multiple CVR numbers. In some cases these look like typos, since only one CVR number exists in virk.dk and the two differ by only one digit. In other cases, all the different CVR numbers exist in virk.dk and the numbers are completely different. I think these correspond to different arms of some larger firm (e.g. the investment arm versus the consulting arm; both listed under the same name in OpenTender but have different CVR numbers).

Of these, I test how many differ within-firm by only 1 digit. This suggests a typo.

7.2 Improved fuzzy matching

We could improve the fuzzy matching by requiring that the second best candidate name is much worse than the first best candidate. This prevents fragile differences from being detected as highly likely matches.

7.3 Updating firm-cvr number key

The matching process relies on a key of firm names and CVR numbers that ends in 2020. I test whether this limits matching by comparing the unmatched rate across tender years. The unmatched rate is the number of candidates without an automatic match divided by all matching candidates in that year. This is more useful than the unmatched count because the number of tenders changes over time.

For KFST, I use the tender publication year. For OpenTender, I use the year of the first contract award publication. This is also the date used in the matching process. OpenTender source rows are counted once, including rows that were later split into several firms. Each panel shows its observed year range. The KFST series begins in 2017 because there are no KFST matching candidates with an earlier publication year.

Unmatched candidates before and after the end of the key
Dataset Period Candidates Unmatched Unmatched rate
KFST Up to 2020 638 104 16.3%
KFST 2021 onwards 163 11 6.7%
OpenTender Up to 2020 20,747 3,655 17.6%
OpenTender 2021 onwards 4,568 791 17.3%

7.4 Verifying data

We could also use the CVR number to name key to verify that the name in the source data is correct. This would help validate the data splitting process I’ve undertaken, for example.
